Observation: Net slip rate and net slip as reported by Kelson and others. (1995). Kelson age estimate based on correlation of glacial deposits. Kelson and others. Evidence for left lateral offset presented, but difficult to assess whether this is tectonic, or a meander in the moraine crest. Vertical displacement is 23 +/- 5 m.

Observation: Slip-rate site is located on a fault direclty south of Antelope Valley fault, that is labled West Walker fault in the USGS Quaternary fault and fold database. The depth profile from the Tahoe outwash terrace gives an age of 149.1+/- 10.5 ka (Rood et al., 2010; Table 1). The two fault scarps offsetting the tread give an estimated summed slip of 42 +8/ -5 and 51+12/-6 m (Figs. S10-S11) and slip rates of 0.3 +/- 0.1 and 0.4 +/-0.1 mm /year, respectively.
Reported or Reinterpreted: average of two reported rates
Full Reference: Rood, D.H., Burbank, D.W. and Finkel, R.C., 2011. Spatiotemporal patterns of fault slip rates across the Central Sierra Nevada frontal fault zone. Earth and Planetary Science Letters, 301(3-4), pp.457-468.

Observation: Slip-rate site is located on a fault direclty south of Antelope Valley fault, that is labled West Walker fault in the USGS Quaternary fault and fold database. The Tenaya moraine gives a 10Be age of 20.3 +/- 2.8 ka (n=8) and offsets from two surveys along the Tenaya moraine crest give calculated slip of 9+4/-1 and 8+3/-1 m (Figs. S12-S13) and a slip rate of 0.4+0.3/-0.1 and 0.4+0.2/-0.1 mm/year.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Full Reference: Rood, D.H., Burbank, D.W. and Finkel, R.C., 2011. Spatiotemporal patterns of fault slip rates across the Central Sierra Nevada frontal fault zone. Earth and Planetary Science Letters, 301(3-4), pp.457-468.

Observation: Slip-rate site is located on a fault direclty south of Antelope Valley fault, that is labled West Walker fault in the USGS Quaternary fault and fold database. The Tioga moraine associated with the southern ice lobe gives a best estimate age of 19.2 +/- 0.6 ka (n=6). Profiles along the moraine crest give slip estimates of 7 +3/-1 and 5+2/-1 m (Figs. S14-S15) and slip rates of 0.4 +0.2/-0.1 and 0.3 +/- 0.1 mm /year, respectively. Samples from another right-lateral Tioga moraine (northern lobe;  lat/long: 38.351917 / -119.469535) yield an age of 19.4 +/- 0.3 ka (n=6) (Table 1). A survey of the offset moraine crest gives a dip-slip estimate of 6+3/-1 m(Fig. S16) and a slip rate of 0.3 +/- 0.1 mm /year.
Reported or Reinterpreted: average of three reported rates
Age Type: 10-Be dating of 6  boulders at each of two sites
Full Reference: Rood, D.H., Burbank, D.W. and Finkel, R.C., 2011. Spatiotemporal patterns of fault slip rates across the Central Sierra Nevada frontal fault zone. Earth and Planetary Science Letters, 301(3-4), pp.457-468.

Site Name: Q5 slip site; 10 km north of Karlsson et al. (2021) paleoseismic trenches
Observation: 11 m of total displacement has been recorded since Q5 time (Axen et al., 1999; Fletchers and Spelz, 2009) at a site 10 km north of Karlsson et al.'s (2021) paleseismic trenches.  Karlsson et al. (2021) use optically stimulated luminescence ages from sediments in the trenches that were collected from below a buried soil they interpret to be correlative with Q5, to estimate a minimum dip-slip rate of ~1.4 mm/yr.  The rate is a mimimum because additional faulting is distributed across a 2.4-km-wide zone (Serna-Hernandez, 2020).

Observation: Slip rate is based on the largest of multiple vertical separation measurements (summed across all strands), within an along-strike group that spans the length of the fault within in the Day Canyon watershed. This rate supercedes the slightly higher rate of Lindvall and Rubin (2006) and is based on McPhillips and Scharer's (2023) recalculation of Lindvall and Rubin's (2006) 10Be dates on boulders, using a the most recent estimate of the 10Be production rate. Conversion of the vertical separation to dip-slip assumes a dip of 33 +/- 8 degrees. The slip rate here is a value that encompasses the individual rates for the past 19 ka, 29 ka, and 40 ka. According to D. McPhillips, this rate supercedes the faster rate from McPhillips and Scharer (2018).
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Offset Type: scarp profiles of offset fan surface
Age Type: 10Be on bouldders, reinterpreted from Lindvall and Rubin (2006)
Full Reference: McPhillips, D. and Scharer, K., 2023. Spatial and Temporal Analysis of Geologic Slip Rates, Cucamonga Fault, California, USA: Implications for Along-Strike Applications and Multi-Fault Rupture. Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth, 128(3), p.e2022JB024942.

Observation: Revised scarp profiling and cosmo dates for the Day Canyon fan site of Morton and Matti (1987). Observation: Alluvial fan surface Qg3a (interpreted as 6-27 ka based on OSL and IRSL ages) is offset 5 m across the intermediate strand and 2.75 m across the western strand of the Black Mountain fault zone.  The eastern strand of the fault zone has has a scarp separating Qg2 from Qg3a but has  not ruptured since Qg3b (4-9 ka)  was deposited. Vertical offset on the eastern strand is not included in the slip-rate estimate.  Sohn et al. (2014) divide the 7.75 m vertical offset of Qg3a across the western and intermediate scarps by the 4-9 ka age of Qg3b to obtain a slip rate of 0.8 - 1.8 mm/yr.  It appears that they discount the possibility that some of the slip measured across these two scarps in Qg3a could have accumulated as early as 27 ka (after Qg3a was deposited and before Qg3b was deposited) because the slopes of the scarps themselves suggest a late Holocene age.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Full Reference: Sohn, M.F., Knott, J.R. and Mahan, S.A., 2014. Paleoseismology of the southern section of the Black Mountains and southern Death Valley fault zones, Death Valley, United States. Environmental and Engineering Geoscience, 20(2), pp.177-198.

Observation: Restoring  240+/-35 m of right-lateral slip aligns a beheaded channel that incises the Qfi surface to the only possible source area along this part of the fault zone. The uncertainty in the offset is based on the width of the channel upstream of the fault.

Observation: Kendrick et al. (2022) interpret Heermance and Yule's (2017) northern strand of the San Gorgonio Pass fault to be the Gandy Ranch fault, and thus report a slip rate of ~1.2 mm/yr for the past 5.7 k.y. and 3.7 mm/yr for the past ~1.3 k.y. on the Gandy Ranch fault.  Per Heermance and Yule (2017): Weighted mean of vertical offset from three profiles on a surface ... is converted to oblique fault slip (e.g., net slip) as a function of fan surface dip, fault dip, and inferred oblique-slip component estimated from lateral offsets [Yule and Sieh, 2003] and regional stress field.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Offset Type: Measured vertical offset across scarp and lateral offset of Qt3/Qt2 terrace riser.
Full Reference: Heermance, R.V. and Yule, D., 2017. Holocene slip rates along the San Andreas Fault System in the San Gorgonio Pass and implications for large earthquakes in southern California. Geophysical Research Letters, 44(11), pp.5391-5400.

Observation: A paleoshoreline of lastest Pleistocene Searles Lake is offset 82 - 106 m (90 m preferred) since the most recent highstand.  Considering that part of the shoreline may have formed in a slighty earlier highstand, the authors use, as a lower bound on the slip rate, the 68-m offset of a channel that clearly incised after the most recent highstand.  The authors make a preliminary calibration of previously published C-14 ages for the most recent highstand to arrive at the slip rates reported here.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Offset Type: offset shoreline (with minimum rate provided by an incised channel)
Full Reference: McGill, S. and Sieh, K., 1993. Holocene slip rate of the central Garlock fault in southeastern Searles Valley, California. Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth, 98(B8), pp.14217-14231.

Observation: This rate is located on the same fan as the rate reported above, but is based on an offset incised channel thalweg. Dating relies on correlation to climatic events, namely the end of the Younger Dryas at ~11.5 ka or the onset of the summer monsoonal pattern at ~8 - 10 ka, which leads to the two alternative slip rates reported by Ganev and others (2012).
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported (two preferred rates with uncertainties listed; selected the rate associated with the onset of summer monsoon patterns--uncertainty associated with this rate includes the rates estimated with Younger Dryas slightly and more with 10Be derived rat
Full Reference: Ganev, P.N., Dolan, J.F., McGill, S.F., and Frankel, K.L., 2012, Constancy of geologic slip rate along the central Garlock fault-Implications for strain accumulation and release in southern California: Geophysical Journal International, https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-246X.2012.05494.x

Observation: Alluvial fan offset 38 +/- 1 m
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Full Reference: Fougere, D., Dolan, J., Rhodes, E. and McGill, S., 2024. Refined Holocene slip rate for the western and central segments of the Garlock fault: record of alternating millennial-scale periods of fast and flow fault slip. Seismica, 3(2).

Observation: Offset channel incised into an offset fan.  Same offset as presented by Ganev et al. (2012), but authors calculate a revised slip rate using new IRSL ages instead the 10Be depth profile age published by Ganev et al. (2012).
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Full Reference: Fougere, D., Dolan, J., Rhodes, E. and McGill, S., 2024. Refined Holocene slip rate for the western and central segments of the Garlock fault: record of alternating millennial-scale periods of fast and flow fault slip. Seismica, 3(2).

Observation: Alluvial fan offset 23 m  (range 19-25 m) from source channel.  Comparing age of  fan to ages of prehistoric earthquakes at the near by El Paso Peaks paleoseismic site shows that the ~23 m offset of the Qal 3 fan at EPM-B was accomplished in the four most recent surface rupturing earthquakes.  Dividing the offset by four times the average recurrence interval during this series of four earthquakes yields a slip rate of 12.5 mm/yr (range 10.3 - 13.6 mm/yr) over the past 3.31 +/- 0.44 ka .
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Full Reference: Burns, James, 2023, Dating of Offset Geomorphic Features Along the Garlock Fault, Mojave Desert, California, M.S. thesis, California State University, San Bernardino, 117 p. https://scholarworks.lib.csusb.edu/etd/1656/

Observation: Stitt (1986) presents three crossections. Two show about 450 m of vertical separation, with as much as 1800 m of vertical separation in the Ramona oil field. The three crossections are within 6 km of each other. 450 m is assumed to be representative along the fault (as the 1800 m value appears to be localized). Age control is based on the fault being entirely...post Saugus. Saugus ages are from Breyer and others (2009), who report the Saugus Formation as 2.6 - 0.78 Ma.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported (author reported as recorded in Appendix B; low and high represented from min and max values listed in Appendix B)

Observation: Reported site coordinates are for the Airport Creek site, which is approximately halfway between the piercing lines used by Hanson and Lettis (1994). The amount of offset is dependant on a paleogeographic reconstruction of the paleo strand line. Hanson and Lettis (1994) report two other, poorly-constrained rates for the Tripod terrace (120 ka) and San Simeon terrace (80, or 105 ka). The reported slip rate for the Tripod terrace is 0.8 - 7.8 m/yr. They report a maximum rate of 8.6 - 11.2 mm/yr for the San Simeon terrace. They also note preferred rate of 1 - 3 mm/yr is consistent with the geomorphic expression of the fault and the shorter term rate reported by Hall and others (1994).
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported (author reported rate as recorded in Appendix B as a maximum rate, thus PrefRate = low end of rante = LowRate)
Short Reference: Hanson and Lettis (1994); Hall et al. (1994)
Full Reference: Hanson, K., and Lettis, W.R., 1994, Estimated Pleistocene slip-rate for the San Simeon fault zone, south-central coastal California, in Alterman, I.B., McMullen, R.B., Cluff, L.S., and Slemmons, D.B., eds., Seismotectonics of the Central California Coast Ranges: Geological Society of America Special Paper 292, p. 133-150. https://doi.org/10.1130/SPE292-p133; Hall, N.T., Hunt, T.D., and Vaughan, P.R., 1994, Holocene behavior of the San Simeon fault zone, south-central California, in Alterman, I.B., McMullen, R.B., Cluff, L.S., and Slemmons, D.B., eds., Seismotectonics of the Central California Coast Ranges: Geological Society of America Special Paper 292, p. 167-189. https://doi.org/10.1130/SPE292-p167

Observation: Offset mid-Pleistocene paleochannels revealed in offset seismic survey.  Channel complex F was mapped across the entire HFZ and its youngest  thalwegs are thought to have been incised during the MIS 8a (ca. 250 ka) or MIS 10a (ca. 345 ka) sea- level low stands and exhibit a total dextral offset of 640 +/- 80 m for a fault slip rate of 2.56 +/- 0.37 mm/yr (95% confidence interval) to 1.86 +/- 0.26 mm/yr. An older MIS 12a (ca. 440 ka) age of incision results in a correspondingly slower slip rate of 1.45 +/- 0.21 mm/yr.

Age Type: Marine Isotope Stages interpreted from seismic stratigraphy

Observation: Authors interpretation of age of incision does not appear to be correct. They use the age of the Qf2 (10 - 128 ka) surface to constrain the slip rate. However, the surface below the riser (Qf1) is Holocene, and would have to be abandoned before slip could start accruing, therefore the offset has to be Holocene age. Based on this, a minimum rate of 1.6 - 2.0 mm/yr is calculated, and is the preferred minimum rate.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported (Appendix B has reinterpreted the age relationships and the reported rate here reflects those reinterpretations).

Site Name: Freeway trench and Indian Hills trench
Observation: Two trenches, a little over a km apart (this is the northernmost site location), together indicate the most recent event occurred ~ 600-900 years ago.  Neither trench exposed clear evidence for a penultimate event, and the freeway trench constrains any penultimate event to have occurred > 9.7 +/- 1.7 ka.   Although the scarp heights the Freeway trench and Indian Hills trenches are 2.3 m and 1.2 m, respectively, the authors use the 0.7 top 0.8 m stratigraphic offsets in the trench and the > 9 k.y. recurrence interval to calculate a slip rate of < 0.1 mm/yr. We report here 0-0.1 mm/yr as the min/max rates.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Full Reference: Pierce, I. K. D., Wesnousky, S. G., Saha, S.,  Moon, S. (2021). Testing the synchronicity of splay-fault ruptures in Carson Valley, Nevada, United States. Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America, 112(2), 704-713.

The focus of this MS thesis was on luminescence dating techniques and alluvial fan ages at various locations in the Coachella Valley, with only limited attention to the slip rate calculation. Fault Name: Mission Ridge - Arroyo Parida - Santa Ana
Observation: Reported geologic rate is a sum of vertical slip rates across fault and folds in Oak View area, per Rockwell and others (1984) Table 3. Summed rate includes Arroyo Parida - Santa Ana, Villanova, and La Vista faults. Rockwell (1984) notes that some of these faults may be flexural slip faults. He also notes that downcutting north of these faults is ~0.8 mm/yr, compared with 1.2 - 2.2 mm/yr in this deformation zone. This would indicate uplift rates of 0.4 - 1.4 mm/yr within this zone of deformation, although it is not clear how much of this uplift is due to faulting at depth, or folding.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported (author reported as recorded in Appendix B; low and high represented from min and max values listed in Appendix B)

Observation: Basedon paleoseismic trenching at Alabama Gates and elsewhere near Lone Pine, the two interseismic intervals between the last three surface-faulting earthquakes on the southern Owens Valley fault are each ~10,000 yr. This ~25,000-year record indicates that the two-event verttical separation of 2.0 m, and, accounitng for the 85-degree dip of the fault, a 2.4 m dip-slip dispolacement.  (The authors call this vertical displacement, but it seems that if the dip of the fault is accounted for, this is a dip-slip displacement, and the reported vertical rate of 0.12 +/- 0.04 mm/yr is really a dip-slip rate.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Full Reference: Bacon, S.N. and Pezzopane, S.K., 2007. A 25,000-year record of earthquakes on the Owens Valley fault near Lone Pine, California: Implications for recurrence intervals, slip rates, and segmentation models. Geological Society of America Bulletin, 119(7-8), pp.823-847.

Observation: Basedon paleoseismic trenching at Alabama Gates and elsewhere near Lone Pine, the two interseismic intervals between the last three surface-faulting earthquakes on the southern Owens Valley fault are each ~10,000 yr. This ~25,000-year record indicates that the two-event dip-slip rate on the Owens Valley fault near Lone Pine of 0.12 +/- 0.04 mm/yr.  The authors convert this to an oblique (right-lateral and normal) slip rate of 1.0 +/- 0.5 m/k.y. using an assumed ratio of right-lateral to vertical slip of 6:1 to 10:1 (based on the mean and maximum ratio measured along the entier 1872 rupture).

Observation: Anderson (1979) reported a long term, Miocene, slip rate of 1 - 2 mm/yr. Petersen and Wesnousky (1994) report 0.3 - 5.3, based on a 9.6 km fanglomerate offset, reported by Dibblee (1968), assuming this offset is Pleistocene and younger. A review of geologic mapping by Dibblee (1967a, b; Dibblee (1968) shows that the interpretation of the 9.6 km offset is problematic, as there are no well-defined piercing lines, or correlative deposits. The deposits (located south of the fault) are also moderately dipping (~20 degrees) and given the proximity to the fault, it is possible the unit Dibblee mapped is an offset fold. In summary, this offset is probably too poorly constrained and documented to be considered useful for deriving a Quaternary slip rate.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported (only range reported/no uncertainty. Though, this rate seems poorly constrained)
Full Reference: Anderson, J.G., 1979, Estimating the seismicity from geological structure for seismic-risk studies: Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America, v. 69, no. 1, p. 135-158. https://doi.org/10.1785/BSSA0690010135; Petersen, M.D., and Wesnousky, S.G., 1994, Fault slip rates and earthquake histories for active faults in Southern California: Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America, v. 84, p. 1608-1649. https://doi.org/10.1785/BSSA0840051608

Observation: Five drainages are intepreted by Kendrick et al. (2015) as matching across the Pinto Mountain fault after restoration of 1-1.25 km of left-lateral slip.  The drainages have not been directely dated, but the Mill Creek fault is interpreted to also be offset the same amount, and the cessation of slip on the Mill Creek fault is interpreted to have occurred around 100 ka, resulting in a left-lateral slip rate for  of 10-12.5 mm/yr for the Pinto Moutnain fault over the past 100 ka.  The ~100 ka age of cessation of slip on the Mill Creek fault is inferred from the the continuity (lack of significant right-lateral offset) of drainages that have eroded headward (northward) across the Mill Creek fault in Raywood flat.  The volume of material eroded north of the Mill Creek fault as a result of this headward erosion 31.5 cubic km) is compared to the erosion rate measured by Binnie et al. (2008) (270 +/- 28 mm/yr) to infer that 106-130 k.y. have elapsed since the headward erosion began, and no right-lateral offset appears to have occurred during this time. In contrast, other studies, without peer-review, have reported lower slip rates: 1.1 to 1.6 mm/yr in the past 11.1 to 14.1 ka at Twenty-nine Palms (Cadena et al., 2004)  and 3 +0.6/-0.4 mm/yr in the past 87 ka at Big Morongo Canyon (Gabriel, 2017).
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Offset Type: Allowable lateral offset of the Mill Creek fault an amount consistent with the ofset of 5 drainages farther northeast.
Age Type: Volume of eroded sediment divided by a measured erosion rate from Binnie et al. (2008).
Full Reference: Kendrick, K.J., Matti, J.C. and Mahan, S.A., 2015. Late Quaternary slip history of the Mill Creek strand of the San Andreas fault in San Gorgonio Pass, southern California: The role of a subsidiary left-lateral fault in strand switching. Bulletin, 127(5-6), pp.825-849.

Observation: Liu-Zeng and others (2006) originally calculated their slip rate using their offsets and then using the event ages at the Bidart site as age control. This is somewhat indirect and the correlation of events between the sites may be uncertain. Assuming this correlation is correct, the slip rate is recalculated from offsets reported by Liu-Zeng and others (2006) and event ages reported by Akciz and others (2010). This is a four interval rate for a 22.1 m offset between AD 1450 - 1475 and AD 1857. Note that the recalculated slip rate is higher than the reported slip rate of Liu-Zeng and others (2006). This rate is much faster than other reported rates in the area, however because the measured offsets are not directly dated, the resulting rate is highly uncertain and other directly dated offsets are favored for the site slip rate.

Observation: A small alluvial fan southwest of fault has been matched with most likely source gullies northeast side of the fault.  McGill et al. (2013) use a trapezoidal pdf for the offset, with a flat topp from 70 and 190 m, tapering to zero at 60 m and 200 m.  The age constaints include both C-14 and OSL agess that are represeted by a trapezoidal pdf with a fl at top between 10.2 and 10.6 ka and that tapers to zero below 9.5 ka and above 12.2 ka.  This yields a pdf for the slip rate with a flat top of equal likelihood from 6.8 to 16.3 mm/yr and with a 95% confi dence interval of 6.3-18.5 mm/yr.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported (95% CI range listed in Appendix B, no pref value)
Full Reference: McGill, S.F., Owen, L.A., Weldon, R.J., II, and Kendrick, K.J., 2012, Latest Pleistocene and Holocene slip rates of the San Andreas fault, Plunge Creek, Southern California-Implications for strain partitioning within the southern San Andreas fault system for the last ~35 k.y. Geological Society of America Bulletin, v. 124. https://doi.org/10.1130/B30647.1

Observation: Terrace riser northeast of fault correlates with an offset channel wall southwest of fault. McGill and others (2013) used a trapezoidal pdf for the offset, with an equal probability that the offset is between 290 m and 510 m with tails tapering to zero probability below 210 m and above 580 m.  They combine this with a traingular probability density function for the age, with peak at 34.9 ka, and with tails that decrease linearly to zero at the outer ends of the 2? error bounds for the three individual dates (32.17- 36.46 ka) on the terrace above the riser. This yields a pdf for the slip rate that has a flat top from 8.3 to 14.5 mm/yr (the most likely range). The 95% confidence interval for the slip rate is 7.0-15.7 mm/yr.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported (95% CI range listed in Appendix B, no pref value)
Full Reference: McGill, S.F., Owen, L.A., Weldon, R.J., II, and Kendrick, K.J., 2012, Latest Pleistocene and Holocene slip rates of the San Andreas fault, Plunge Creek, Southern California-Implications for strain partitioning within the southern San Andreas fault system for the last ~35 k.y. Geological Society of America Bulletin, v. 124. https://doi.org/10.1130/B30647.1

Full Reference: Clark, M.M., Harms, K.K., Lienkaemper, J.J., Harwood, D.S., Lajoie, K.R., Matti, J.C., Perkins, J.A., Rymer, M.J., Sarna-Wojcicki, A.M., Sharp, R.V., Sims, J.D., Tinsley, J.C., III, and Ziony, J.I., 1984, Preliminary slip rate table and map of late Quaternary faults of California: U.S. Geological Survey Open-File Report 84-106, 12 p., 5 plates, scale 1:1,000,000. https://doi.org/10.3133/ofr84106

Observation: High-angle scarps in late Pleistocene alluvial and lacustrine surfaces along a range-front fault (previously named the Tank Canyon fault zone [Clark et al., 1984; Wesnousky, 1986]) on the west side of the Slate Range are found to be the near-surface expression of slip on a bedrock-rooted low-angle normal fault beneath Searles Valley.  Numelin et al. (2007) refer to both the high-angle and low-angle parts of the fault system as the Searles Valley fault system. Net slip from fault scarp profiles across Qf2 (which slightly post-dates the most recent highstand of Searles Lake) with the central portion of fault zone (between Layton Canyon and New York Canyon) is resolved onto a 20 degree west-dipping detachment beneath Searles Valley yielding 4.4 +/- 0.8 m of dip-slip in the past 15-17 ka.  This age range is based on C-14 dates on tufa from deposits at the most recent shoreline of Searles Lake and an inference (basedon Lin et al., 1998) that these dates may underestimate the true age by 1-2 ka.  Any slip between bedrock and the alluvial deposits where the Searles Valley fault surfaces at the range front will add to the reported slip-rate estimate.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Offset Type: vertical offset along high-angle scarps projected to dip-slip on underlying low-angle normal fault
Age Type: C-14 dating of lacustrine tufa with an inference that true ages are 1-2 ka older.
Full Reference: Numelin, T., Kirby, E., Walker, J.D. and Didericksen, B., 2007. Late Pleistocene slip on a low-angle normal fault, Searles Valley, California. Geosphere, 3(3), pp.163-176.

Observation: Net slip from fault scarp profiles across Qf4o and Qf4y (both of which are inferred to be < 10 ka) along the northern portion of Tank Canyon/Searles Valley fault system (between Sand Canyon and Tank Canyon) is resolved onto a 20 degree west-dipping detachment beneath Searles Valley yielding 2.3 +/- 0.6 m of dip-slip in the past 10 ka.  This age range is based on C-14 dates on tufa from deposits at the most recent shoreline of Searles Lake and an inference (basedon Lin et al., 1998) that these dates may underestimate the true age by 1-2 ka.  Any slip between bedrock and the alluvial deposits where the Searles Valley fault surfaces at the range front will add to the reported slip-rate estimate.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported
Offset Type: vertical offset along high-angle scarps projected to dip-slip on underlying low-angle normal fault
Full Reference: Numelin, T., Kirby, E., Walker, J.D. and Didericksen, B., 2007. Late Pleistocene slip on a low-angle normal fault, Searles Valley, California. Geosphere, 3(3), pp.163-176.

Observation: Poorly-constrained slip rate. Small offset suggests a limited number of events, and the dating constraints are based on soil development correlated to other surfaces. Other slip rates in Coppersmith (1979) and Clark and others (1984) are reported in the 0.1 - 1.3 mm/yr range, but are also poorly constrained, particularly one that is based on a partial offset that is less than 2.4 m, and relies on assumptions of hz:v to derive a slip rate.
Reported or Reinterpreted: reported (author reported as recorded in Appendix B; low and high represented from min and max values listed in Appendix B)
Full Reference: Coppersmith, K.J., 1979, Activity assessment of the Zayante-Vergeles fault, central San Andreas fault system, California: Santa Cruz, University of California, Ph.D. dissertation, 210 p.
